notes: "We selected functional traits (Table 2) that explicitly characterize the grazing interaction between phytoplankton and zooplankton, as well as their interaction with other trophic levels in the food web. For phytoplankton, we selected traits that define how they interface with resources (i.e. nutrients and light) and the zooplankton grazers (motility, edibility, colony formation). For zooplankton, we focused on traits that define how they consume phytoplankton (i.e. feeding type and trophic group). Phytoplankton traitvalues were obtained from a literature review (see (Longhi and Beisner 2010 for details) and included (i)capacity for N-fixation, (ii) silica demand, (iii) capacity for mixotrophy, (iv) pigment composition (v) cellmotility and (vi) edibility to zooplankton (>35 μm linear dimension) and (vii) tendency to form colonies." They also looked at genus level taxonomy data.

We combined related taxa into higher taxonomic groups focusing our analysis on 5 major groups: (1) Chlorophyta;
(2) Chrysophyta, including diatoms; (3) Cryptophyta; (4)
Cyanophyta; and (5) Pyrrhophyta. Zooplankton length-weight, taxonomy. Because we were more interested in the functional
aspects of different zooplankton taxa, especially the
potential for top-down control of phytoplankton biomass through zooplankton herbivory, we used a proposed
functional classification scheme (Barnett et al. 2007) to
aggregate our taxonomic groupings into 5 main functional groups: (1) littoral and pelagic small herbivorous
cladocerans and calanoid and cyclopoid copepods; (2) the
remaining omnivorous and carnivorous copepods; (3)
large pelagic cladocerans; (4) large littoral cladocerans;
and (5) Diaphanosoma. Identified crustacean zooplankton
taxa that were not functionally categorized were assigned
to the most likely functional group based on the criteria in
Barnett et al. (2007) and the functional ecological descriptions in Balcer et al. (1984). Identified taxa that were enumerated but not functionally categorized by Barnett et
al. (2007; i.e., rotifers and dreissenid veliger larvae) were
assigned to new functional groups: (1) omnivorous rotifers, (2) predacious rotifers, and (3) dreissenid veligers.
